title: Read the Receipt, Not the Tea Leaves
context: The team is assessing the OCR receipt-scanning capability for the Expenses module. A vendor has just demoed a polished mobile capture flow. Tax wants HMRC-relevant detail. Security wants to understand how evidence is stored. Product wants the experience to feel almost effortless for employees. You are the solution architect. The Programme Director is chairing.

questions.prompt: The OCR vendor says: "Our platform handles the full pipeline end to end: capture, extraction, field mapping, and claim creation. You just call our API and we return a completed claim object. Your system does not need its own extraction logic or confidence model." Engineering is attracted to the simplicity. How should you respond?

options.text: That is the fastest path to delivery. If the vendor handles capture through to claim creation, we avoid building extraction logic, confidence scoring, and field mapping ourselves. We can treat the vendor as a black box and focus our engineering effort on the rest of the module.
options.explanation: Delegating the entire pipeline to a vendor creates a black-box dependency at a critical point in the claim journey. The system needs its own confidence model to decide when to highlight fields for user confirmation, its own fallback path when images cannot be interpreted, and its own mapping logic to connect extracted data to internal domain concepts like payslip items and VAT treatment. Outsourcing all of that means the product cannot control the user experience at the point where accuracy matters most.

options.text: We should own the confidence model and the user intervention logic even if we use a vendor for raw text extraction. The product needs to decide when to highlight low-confidence fields for confirmation, how to prompt the user to retake a photo or enter details manually, and how to map extracted values to our own domain model. Those are product behaviours, not vendor behaviours. The extraction engine is a replaceable input; the decision and mapping layer is ours.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it draws a clean boundary between commodity extraction and domain-specific logic. The vendor can provide OCR and text extraction, but confidence thresholds, user intervention paths, fallback behaviour, and domain mapping must be owned by the product because they determine the quality and reliability of the claim creation experience.

options.text: We should avoid the vendor entirely and build the OCR pipeline in-house. Receipt scanning is a core capability of the module, and outsourcing it creates a strategic dependency we will regret as the feature matures and we need more control over accuracy and speed.
options.explanation: Building raw OCR and text extraction in-house is a significant investment in a commodity capability. The architectural principle is to use external services for commodity extraction while owning the domain logic that sits above it. The decision is not build-everything versus buy-everything; it is about drawing the right boundary between replaceable infrastructure and product-specific behaviour.

questions.prompt: The Tax Lead says: "For VAT compliance, the extracted data needs to include VAT amount, VAT rate, supplier VAT registration number where present, and the split between taxable and non-taxable elements. That is a lot of structure from a receipt photo." Engineering asks whether the OCR output schema should be kept minimal and let downstream services infer the tax treatment. How should you respond?

options.text: The OCR output should stay minimal. Extracting merchant, date, total, and line items is already challenging enough from receipt images. Tax classification is a business rules problem, not an image processing problem, and should be handled by a downstream tax service that knows the HMRC rules.
options.explanation: This separates extraction from VAT detection in a way that loses information. Some VAT-relevant data is only available on the receipt itself, such as the supplier's VAT registration number and the explicit VAT breakdown. If the extraction schema does not capture these fields, a downstream service cannot reconstruct them without re-processing the image.

options.text: The extraction schema needs to capture the VAT-relevant fields at scan time because that information is on the receipt and will not be available later unless we store and re-process the image. The OCR output model should include VAT amount, VAT rate, supplier VAT registration number where present, and the taxable versus non-taxable split. A downstream service can then validate and apply HMRC rules, but it needs the raw extracted values to work from.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it recognises that the extraction schema must be rich enough to support downstream compliance processing. The architectural point is that the OCR pipeline is the only stage with direct access to the receipt image, so any data that originates from the physical receipt must be captured in the extraction output rather than deferred to a service that only sees structured data.

options.text: We should store the original receipt image and let a specialised tax service re-read it later with its own OCR pass focused specifically on VAT fields. That way the initial extraction stays fast and simple, and the tax service can apply its own purpose-built model to find what it needs.
options.explanation: Running two separate OCR passes over the same image adds latency, cost, and complexity without a clear benefit. The extraction pipeline should produce a single rich output that captures all required fields in one pass. The image should be stored as evidence, but it should not be the primary mechanism for tax-relevant data extraction.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Lead says: "OCR extraction should produce a generic key-value output. Mapping to payslip items, expense categories, and VAT treatment should happen in a separate downstream service. That keeps the OCR component clean and reusable." The Payroll analyst says they need the mapping to happen before the employee sees the claim. How should you respond?

options.text: A generic key-value output is the right first step. If we keep OCR decoupled from domain concepts, we can reuse the extraction component across other modules in future. The mapping service can consume the output and enrich it before claim creation, and the employee will not notice the difference.
options.explanation: While separation of concerns is generally sound, the timing matters here. The mapping to payslip items and claim type needs to happen before the employee reviews the pre-populated claim, because the goal is to reduce claim creation effort end to end. If the employee has to classify the claim manually after scanning because the mapping has not run yet, the feature loses most of its value.

options.text: The extraction and domain mapping need to complete as a single pipeline before the claim is presented to the employee. The employee should see a pre-populated claim with the correct type and category already resolved, not a bag of raw fields they have to classify themselves. We can still separate extraction from mapping internally as distinct responsibilities, but from the user's perspective the pipeline must complete before they review the result.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it recognises that architectural separation does not mean the steps can happen at different lifecycle stages. Extraction and mapping can be distinct services, but they must execute as a synchronous pipeline that completes before the claim is shown to the employee. The system should automatically map OCR output to the relevant payslip item so the employee does not have to select the claim type manually.

options.text: We should combine extraction and mapping into a single monolithic service to guarantee they always execute together. Separating them just creates an integration point that can fail and adds latency to the scan experience.
options.explanation: Collapsing the two concerns into one service trades architectural clarity for a coupling guarantee that can be achieved more cleanly with a synchronous pipeline. Keeping extraction and mapping as distinct responsibilities makes each one independently testable, replaceable, and evolvable, while a pipeline orchestrator ensures they always run in sequence before presenting results.

questions.prompt: The Security Architect says: "To reduce risk, can we process receipt images transiently and discard them once the text has been extracted? Storing images creates another class of sensitive data we need to govern." How should you respond?

options.text: Yes. If the structured data has already been captured, keeping the image is unnecessary. Throwing images away after OCR is the cleanest security posture and customers are unlikely to miss them.
options.explanation: That is incompatible with the requirement. All scanned receipts and the extracted data are meant to be stored securely and automatically attached to the expense claim. The evidence itself is part of the claim record.

options.text: We should absolutely govern the storage carefully, but secure retention is part of the feature rather than an optional extra. The requirement is that scanned receipts and their extracted data are stored securely and attached to the claim. So the architecture question is how we protect and retain that evidence properly, not whether we keep it at all.
options.explanation: This is the correct response. It respects the explicit storage requirement while acknowledging the legitimate security concern and reframing it as a design obligation rather than a reason to discard the evidence.

options.text: We can keep the image only for receipts where VAT has been detected, and discard it for simpler claims. That gives Finance the audit evidence they need without forcing us to store every receipt equally.
options.explanation: The requirement is broader than that. It says all scanned receipts and the extracted data are stored securely and attached to the claim, not only the subset that meets a VAT condition.

questions.prompt: The Data Architect says: "Duplicate detection should sit in the analytics layer where we have the full claim history and can run sophisticated matching. Putting detection logic inside the OCR pipeline means maintaining duplicate-matching code in two places once anomaly detection arrives." How should you respond?

options.text: That is architecturally cleaner. If anomaly detection will eventually own pattern matching across the claim history, building a separate duplicate check inside OCR creates redundancy. We should accept the short-term gap and wait for the analytics layer to handle it properly.
options.explanation: Deferring duplicate detection to an analytics layer means duplicates enter the system and are only caught after submission, review, or even approval. The OCR requirement is specifically about alerting the user at the point of entry when the receipt appears similar to an existing claim. That is a different concern from post-hoc anomaly detection and serves a different purpose: preventing accidental resubmission before it creates downstream work.

options.text: These are two different concerns operating at different points in the lifecycle. Submission-time duplicate detection alerts the employee before they complete a claim, which prevents accidental resubmission and reduces noise for managers. Analytics-layer anomaly detection operates after the fact across the full claim population to find suspicious patterns. Both are needed, and they do not duplicate each other because they serve different users at different times with different response models.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it separates the two concerns by lifecycle stage and purpose. Submission-time detection is a user-facing guard that prevents obvious mistakes. Analytics-layer detection is a back-office capability that identifies statistical anomalies. Conflating them would leave a gap at the point of entry where the user can still be helped.

options.text: We should build duplicate detection only at submission time and skip the analytics-layer version entirely. If the OCR pipeline catches duplicates before they enter the system, there is no need for a second check downstream.
options.explanation: Submission-time detection catches obvious duplicates based on image or field similarity, but it cannot detect the kinds of cross-claim patterns that anomaly detection is designed to find, such as systematic over-claiming or coordinated duplicates across different employees. Both layers serve distinct purposes.
